title: The Long Way Round
setup: Spot marker at ladder base (start) and slide exit (finish)
Stand near the rope grid descent on first run

activity_flow.instruction: Start at ladder base. Child looks at ladder.
Child climbs ladder to ladder mid. Don't prompt mid-climb.
activity_flow.prompt: Wait. Only act if child signals distress.

activity_flow.instruction: Pause at ladder mid. Child spots the rope grid.
activity_flow.prompt: "What can you see? This part is wobbly — how will you hold on?"

activity_flow.instruction: Traverse rope grid laterally to rope grid mid.
Descend rope grid to ground. Face apparatus, one foot at a time.
Walk outside: rope grid base → outside corner → inside entrance.
activity_flow.prompt: Ask child to recall where they've been. What comes next?

activity_flow.instruction: Enter inside the apparatus to inside platform.
Climb steps inside to slide platform.
Child pauses, then slides to slide exit.

activity_flow.prompt: "Can you remember every part of the journey?"

adult_guidance.headline: Narrate, don't catch
adult_guidance.detail: Stay close on the rope grid descent but spot with eyes. Let the child find their own footing.

adult_guidance.headline: Say the words before they move
adult_guidance.detail: Over, across, through, up, down, inside, outside. Position language before the movement, not as a correction after.

adult_guidance.headline: Pause cue, not warning
adult_guidance.detail: If they rush: "Stop — what's your plan?" Builds motor planning without implying danger.

adult_guidance.headline: Walks are load-bearing
adult_guidance.detail: Ground sections are sequencing time. Don't skip them. Ask what comes next.

adult_guidance.headline: Celebrate the decision, not the climb
adult_guidance.detail: "I noticed you slowed down on the ropes" lands better than "good climbing."

variations: Reverse: start at slide exit, climb steps, go back through in opposite order
Add crawl: detour through crawl hole between inside platform and steps
Memory run: adult names the route once, then stays silent — child leads
free_play: Make your own long way round
Use at least one climb and one walk
Start and end at different spots
why_it_works: The route mixes a ladder ascent, rope traverse, rope descent, ground walk, enclosed transition, and slide — no two consecutive movements use the same motor pattern, forcing continuous motor planning. The walk sections require the child to hold the full route in memory without apparatus cues, making sequencing cognitive as well as physical. The adult's role is to make the child's planning visible through language, not to remove the need for it.
